q: Alpha decay ($\alpha$ decay) is a process in which an atomic nucleus emits an ___ (helium nucleus).
In alpha decay, the mass number reduces by four and the atomic number reduces by ___.
___ and mass number are conserved in the decay process.
$\beta^-$ decay (beta-minus decay) is a process in which a ___ in an atomic nucleus changes to a proton.
In $\beta^-$ decay, the parent nucleus transforms into the daughter nucleus, with mass number remains unchanged but atomic number increases by ___.
$\beta^+$ decay (beta-plus decay) is a process in which a ___ in an atomic nucleus changes to a neutron.
Positron, the ___ of electron, has the same mass and same magnitude of electric charge as an electron.
The existence of positron was theoretically predicted by Dirac in 1931 and experimentally detected by ___ in 1932.
Neutrino, a fundamental particle as an electron, has no charge and negligibly small ___.
For a nucleus to be stable, there must be an appropriate ratio of ___ and neutrons.
If a nucleus has excess neutrons, ___ takes place.
A nucleus with excess protons undergoes ___.
In gamma decay ($\gamma$ decay), the daughter nucleus resulting from alpha or beta decay is left in an ___ state.
In gamma decay, the atomic number and mass number of the parent and daughter nuclei remain ___.
The ___ is the quantum of the electromagnetic field.
Photons are massless and move at the ___ in vacuum.
The energy of a photon is ___ proportional to the wavelength of the electromagnetic radiation associated with it.
Alpha rays consist of ___ nuclei, positively charged particles.
The charge of an $\alpha$ particle is ___.
Alpha rays are the ___ ionizing of the three rays.
Alpha rays are the ___ penetrating of the three rays.
Alpha rays can be stopped by a thick sheet of ___.
Beta rays consist of either electrons or ___ moving with varying speeds.
Beta rays can be stopped by a few millimetres of ___.
___ are electromagnetic waves like light and X-rays, but have much shorter wavelengths.
Intensity of gamma rays can be greatly reduced by several centimetres of ___ but they are never completely stopped.
The rate of decay of a radioactive sample is called ___.
SI unit for activity is ___ ($Bq$).
A unit of activity still being used today is ___ ($Ci$).
The rate of decay (activity) is a characteristic of the radioactive element and it is unaffected by the ___.
The ___ is defined as the time for half the atoms in a radioactive sample to decay.
Radioactive decay is a ___ process: it is impossible to predict when a particular atom will decay.
A ___ is a small amount of radioactive isotope introduced into a system in order to follow the behaviour of some components of that system.
In medicine, radioactive tracers are effectively used in ___ procedures.
Treatment with radiation is known as ___.
Radioisotopes such as ___ and cesium-137 are widely used to treat cancer in radiotherapy.
In industrial gamma radiography, radioactive materials are used to inspect ___ or welded joints.
The ability to use radioisotopes to accurately measure thickness is widely used as ___ system in the production of sheet materials.
___ is a technique for estimating the age of an object by measuring the amounts of various radioisotopes present in it.
Carbon dating was developed by ___.
There is one carbon-14 atom in every trillion ($10^{12}$) of ___ atoms.
___ is the stable final decay product of a series of decays that starts with uranium.
Gamma and neutron irradiation of seeds can be used to induce ___ to develop desired mutants.
